ontology.foodex2_description: Snow peas (Pisum sativum var. saccharatum), raw

main_ingredients: snow pea pods (Pisum sativum var. saccharatum)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw snow peas is approximately 98 g (about 1 cup trimmed), commonly eaten as a snack, crudité, or added to salads.

nutrient_source.title: Snow peas, raw — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for snow pea, raw (cr_id F09020024). Secondary cross-reference with USDA FoodData Central NDB 11300 (peas, edible-podded, raw) confirms macro alignment. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrient detail supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy ID 11300 where HPB does not report those fields.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Snow peas (Pisum sativum var. saccharatum) are not individually listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 22 is estimated from the closest analogous approved-source entry: green peas (Pisum sativum), GI = 22 (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Snow peas share the same species, similar carbohydrate density (4.7 g/100 g), and high fibre content (2.6 g/100 g), supporting this analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (22 × 4.7 × 0.98) / 100 ≈ 1.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium (1 mg/100 g) and high potassium (420 mg/100 g) yielding an exceptional sodium-to-potassium ratio of approximately 1:420
hypertension.guidance: Snow peas are an excellent DASH-diet vegetable; their negligible sodium and high potassium content actively support blood pressure reduction, and they should be consumed without high-sodium sauces to preserve this benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (4.7 g/100 g), high dietary fibre (2.6 g/100 g), and estimated low GI of 22 supporting postprandial blood glucose stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Snow peas have a low glycemic index (~22) and low glycemic load (~1.0 per 98 g serving); their fibre and protein content slow glucose absorption, making them a suitable vegetable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes when prepared without sugar-based sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.04 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high potassium (420 mg/100 g), and dietary fibre (2.6 g/100 g) supporting lipid and vascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Snow peas contribute negligible saturated fat and cholesterol while providing potassium and soluble fibre that support healthy blood pressure and LDL cholesterol levels, making them a cardioprotective vegetable suitable for daily inclusion in a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Provides vitamin C (60 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome immune modulation, but lacks omega-3 fatty acids and other primary anti-inflammatory nutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Snow peas offer antioxidant vitamin C and gut-supportive fibre that may modestly benefit rheumatoid arthritis management, but they should be paired with omega-3-rich foods such as salmon or walnuts for meaningful anti-inflammatory effect; avoid cooking with high-saturated-fat oils or high-sodium sauces.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Snow pea (Pisum sativum var. saccharatum), consumed raw, is a flat-podded leguminous vegetable with tiny, underdeveloped peas visible inside the crisp, tender, bright-green pod. Unlike garden peas, the entire pod is edible. Raw snow peas have a mild, slightly sweet flavour with a satisfying crunch. They are widely used in East and Southeast Asian cuisines — particularly Chinese stir-fries, Singaporean vegetable dishes, and Japanese salads — as well as in Western crudité platters and salads. Nutritionally, raw snow peas are low in calories (38 kcal/100 g) and carbohydrates (4.7 g/100 g), with a meaningful protein content of 3.0 g/100 g for a vegetable, 2.6 g dietary fibre, and an outstanding potassium-to-sodium ratio (420 mg potassium vs 1 mg sodium per 100 g). They are an excellent source of vitamin C (~60 mg/100 g), providing antioxidant support. Their estimated glycemic index of approximately 22 (low) makes them suitable for blood glucose management. Snow peas are naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Clinically, they are well-suited for individuals managing hypertension (DASH diet), type 2 diabetes (low GI, high fibre), and cardiovascular disease (negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol). In Singapore, snow peas are commonly known as 荷兰豆 (hé lán dòu) in Chinese and kacang salji in Malay.
common_user_queries: Are snow peas good for diabetes?
Snow pea vs sugar snap pea nutrition
Can I eat snow peas raw?
Are snow peas good for blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F09020024, confidence 0.95), cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy ID 11300 (edible-podded peas, raw). Fatty acid detail supplemented from USDA SR Legacy. GI estimated from closest approved analogous source (green peas, GI 22, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). All values are internally consistent and plausible for raw snow peas.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.04 g), monounsaturated fat (0.02 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.09 g), trans_fat_g (0.0 g), vitamin C (60 mg), vitamin A (54 mcg RAE), B-vitamins, vitamin D (0.0 g), vitamin E (0.39 mg), phosphorus (53 mg), magnesium (24 mg), zinc (0.27 mg), selenium (0.7 mcg), and water (88.9 g) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy ID 11300 (peas, edible-podded, raw) where HPB does not report these fields. 2. Cholesterol is confirmed zero (0.0 mg) — naturally zero for a plant-based food. Vitamin B12 is confirmed zero (0.0 mcg) — naturally zero for a plant food. Vitamin D is confirmed zero (0.0 mcg) — naturally zero for a raw vegetable. Trans fat is confirmed zero (0.0 g) — naturally zero for a raw plant food. 3. GI of 22 is an analogous estimate based on green peas (Pisum sativum) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); snow peas are not individually listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Food group corrected from input 'Meat and alternatives' to 'Vegetables' — snow peas are a podded vegetable, not a meat alternative in the Singapore HPB classification context. 5. The input health_suitability entry for rheumatoid_arthritis listed suitable: false with a contradictory reason citing anti-inflammatory properties; this has been corrected to suitable: true with concern_level moderate and an accurate clinical note. 6. The input dietary_tag 'keto' has been removed as snow peas at 4.7 g carbohydrate/100 g are borderline for strict ketogenic diets and the tag is not supported by HPB classification.
